Off Calendar Apr-23-2026 Continued To Jul-01-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE
Off Calendar May-19-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT JAWID SAFI'S MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Jawid Safi's motion to transfer venue of this action to the San Diego Superior Court is granted pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure, section 397, subdivision (c). Safi shall pay any costs of transfer.

Safi moves based on the mandatory venue transfer provisions of Code of Civil Procedure sec. 395 and sec. 397, subdivision (a). The court does not grant relief on this ground. Plaintiff's choice of venue is presumptively correct, and defendant has the burden of showing that plaintiff commenced the action in an improper court. (Mission Imports, Inc. v. Superior Court (1982) 31 Cal.3d 921, 928 ["[I]n the absence of an affirmative showing to the contrary, the presumption is that the county in which the title of the action shows that it is brought is, prima facie, the proper county for the commencement and trial of the action."] [citation simplified].) Venue is proper in San Francisco because defendant Uber Technologies, Inc., has its principal place of business here. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 395.5.) Safi does not meet his burden under this statute.

Safi alternatively moves under Code of Civil Procedure, sec. 397, subdivision (c), which permits the court to transfer venue "[w]hen the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the change." The court considers only the convenience of nonparty witnesses and requires the moving party to make an affirmative showing of where the witnesses are located and what testimony they are expected to offer. Safi has made an evidentiary showing here: He provides a police report showing that the police report was written by a San Diego Police Department officer and that plaintiff Kelsey Carson was transported to a San Diego hospital, indicating that her treatment providers are located in or near San Diego. Although Uber is located in San Francisco and its employees may be witnesses to the corporate policies plaintiffs have put at issue, the convenience of parties is not considered on this motion, and in any event Uber has not opposed the motion. Safi does not offer witness statements, but the court makes common-sense assumptions about the relevance of emergency responder and treating provider testimony to the trial. Other passengers in the vehicle are listed as residents of San Bernardino County, which is much closer to San Diego County than to San Francisco.

The court also considers the ends of justice. "[W]here a venue is clearly more convenient to most of the witnesses, that alone supports an inference that moving the trial promotes the interests of justice because delay and expense in court proceedings are avoided and savings in the witnesses' time and expenses are effected." (Rycz v. Superior Court of San Francisco County (2022) 81 Cal.App.5th 824, 848 [citation simplified].) In addition, "conducting trials in the venue most connected to the underlying events contributes to a more fair and efficient system of justice." (Id., at p. 848.) Although Uber's policies are likely formulated in San Francisco, the same was true in Rycz. Moreover, plaintiffs concede that neither plaintiff is a resident of San Francisco County, and plaintiff Kelsey Carson has received medical care in the Inland Empire and Ohio. The court concludes that the ends of justice favor granting Safi's motion.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)
